title: The CIA triad and how controls support it

sections.body: Almost every security decision traces back to three goals: keeping data secret, keeping it accurate, and keeping it available. Those goals are the CIA triad — Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability — and they give you a quick way to reason about why a control exists and what it protects.

sections.heading: What each goal means
sections.body: The three goals often pull in different directions, so security is usually about balancing them rather than maximizing one.
bullets: Confidentiality: only authorized people can read the data (e.g., encryption, access control).
Integrity: data is not changed without authorization, and changes are detectable (e.g., hashing, signatures).
Availability: the data and systems are reachable when needed (e.g., redundancy, backups).

sections.heading: Controls fill the gaps
sections.body: Controls are how you achieve the triad, and they come in categories — technical, managerial, operational, and physical — and types such as preventive, detective, and corrective. A locked door is a physical preventive control; a log review is a detective control. When you meet a new control, ask which triad goal it serves and whether it prevents, detects, or corrects.
Cryptography shows up everywhere in security, but it is built from a small number of primitives. Knowing what each one does — and what it does not do — keeps you from reaching for the wrong tool.

sections.heading: Encryption: symmetric and asymmetric
sections.body: Encryption protects confidentiality by making data unreadable without a key.
bullets: Symmetric uses one shared key for both encrypt and decrypt. It is fast, so it protects bulk data like a disk (e.g., AES).
Asymmetric uses a public/private key pair. It is slower, so it is used for key exchange and digital signatures (e.g., RSA, ECC).

sections.heading: Hashing and signatures
sections.body: Hashing is one-way: it produces a fixed-length fingerprint that detects any change, giving you integrity — but it is not encryption and does not hide data. A digital signature combines a hash with the signer's private key, so the recipient gets integrity plus non-repudiation: proof of who sent it. A useful rule of thumb: encrypt for secrecy, hash for integrity, sign for proof of origin.

title: Social engineering and malware in plain terms

sections.body: Many breaches do not start with clever code — they start with a convincing message. Social engineering manipulates people into bypassing security, and it pairs naturally with malware that does the technical damage once a foothold exists.

sections.heading: Social engineering by channel
sections.body: The lure is the same; only the delivery changes.
bullets: Phishing: deceptive email; spear phishing targets specific people; whaling targets executives.
Vishing: voice/phone; smishing: SMS text.
Common levers: urgency, authority, fear, and familiarity.

sections.heading: Malware by behavior
sections.body: Classify malware by what it does, not just its name.
bullets: Ransomware encrypts data and demands payment.
Spyware secretly collects information; a keylogger records keystrokes.
A worm self-propagates; a trojan hides inside something useful; a rootkit hides its own presence.

sections.body: The strongest defense blends technical controls with people: filtering and endpoint protection reduce what reaches users, while awareness training reduces how often a lure succeeds.
Data is not protected by a single switch. It lives in three states, and each one needs its own controls. Designing for all three is what keeps information safe end to end.

sections.heading: The three states
sections.body: Match the control to the state.
bullets: At rest (storage): full-disk or file/database encryption protects lost or stolen media.
In transit (network): TLS or a VPN tunnel protects data crossing untrusted links.
In use (memory): access controls and trusted execution limit exposure while data is processed.

sections.heading: Classify first, then protect
sections.body: You cannot protect what you have not labeled. Data classification — Public, Internal, Confidential, and so on — tells you how strongly each item must be protected. Techniques like tokenization (swapping data for unrelated tokens) and masking further limit exposure of sensitive fields where full encryption is not practical.

When something goes wrong, a calm, repeatable process beats improvisation. Incident response gives teams a shared sequence so nothing critical is skipped under pressure — and so the organization actually learns from each event.

sections.heading: The phases in order
sections.body: A widely used model runs in six phases.
bullets: Preparation: tools, plans, and training before anything happens.
Identification: detect and confirm that an incident is real.
Containment: limit the spread and damage.
Eradication: remove the cause (malware, compromised accounts).
Recovery: restore systems and verify they are clean.
Lessons learned: document what happened and improve.

sections.heading: Where teams slip
sections.body: Two phases get shortchanged. Containment is sometimes rushed straight to eradication, which can tip off an attacker or destroy evidence. And lessons learned is often skipped once the fire is out — yet that is the step that prevents the next incident. Treat documentation as part of the job, not an afterthought.

Security cannot eliminate every risk, so governance is about making deliberate, documented choices. A risk-management program identifies what could go wrong, decides how to handle it, and tracks those decisions over time.

sections.heading: Four ways to handle a risk
sections.body: Every identified risk gets a treatment decision.
bullets: Mitigate: reduce likelihood or impact with controls.
Transfer: shift the financial impact, e.g., insurance.
Avoid: stop doing the risky activity.
Accept: formally tolerate a low, documented risk.

sections.heading: Putting numbers on risk
sections.body: Quantitative analysis helps justify spending. The single loss expectancy (SLE) is the cost of one event; the annualized rate of occurrence (ARO) is how often it happens per year; and the annualized loss expectancy is ALE = SLE x ARO. If a control costs less than the ALE it removes, it is usually worth it. All of these decisions belong in a risk register — a living list of risks with owners, ratings, and treatments — so the program stays accountable.
